text: Laparoscopic labs procedure for intestinal malrotation in newborns and children. A twelve-day-old male was referred to our institution complaining of biliary vomit after feeding since day five. Labs were within normal limits. Radiographs showed distal pneumatization. An upper gastrointestinal series revealed a double bubble and no contrast in distal duodenum. So, a duodenal atresia was suspected and the patient underwent a diagnostic laparoscopy.

Under general anesthesia, the patient is positioned in a frog-like posture. The surgeon makes an inframilical incision and inserts a 5mm trocar using the Hasson technique. Two additional 3mm trocars are inserted under direct vision in the right flank and left hypochondrium. An extracorporeal stitch is used to elevate the round ligament, and a similar technique is applied to suspend the right hepatic lobe.

The surgeon identifies a distended duodenum and transects peritoneal bands between the liver, retroperitoneum, and bowel. The superior mesenteric artery is visible. After retracting and lifting the duodenum, a duodenal volvulus is observed along an edematous mesenteric stalk. The cecum and appendix are seen in a high and medial position below the liver, and the volvulus is undone.

The surgeon identifies an internal para duodenal hernia and carefully dissects and removes the hernia sac. An appendectomy is performed by skeletonizing and cauterizing the appendicular artery, then exteriorizing the appendix through the umbilical incision for extracorporeal completion. The cecum is returned to the abdominal cavity, and the mesentery is widened. The small bowel is positioned on the right side and the colon on the left side, confirming correct anatomical alignment.

From August, two thousand and fourteen to January, two thousand and sixteen, five patients have had laparoscopic latch procedure for intestinal malrotation in our institution. We found that all the patients had at least one episode of bowel obstruction before surgery. Three patients had internal hernia. Four patients underwent laparoscopic appendectomy, one extracorporeal. There were no conversions to open procedure and no complications. All the patients started oral liquids on day four with good tolerance. In our study we found A mean age of two point nine years. A mean weight of eleven point nine kilograms. Two patients had initial diagnosis of intestinal malrotation. One patient of duodenal atresia and two patients of bowel obstruction. Mean surgery time and hospital stay were one hundred and twenty minutes and nine days respectively. Intestinal malrotation is a differential diagnosis that should be considered in all pediatric patients with bowel obstruction symptoms. Laparoscopy is a good tool to reach a final diagnosis. Laparoscopic latch procedure is a feasible option for patients with intestinal malrotation with or without internal hernia. It can be performed in newborns and children sexually with a short hospital stay.
